frameworks.name: deep meta-learning reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: RL agents meta-trained to find shortest paths in novel environments.

frameworks.name: transition-based navigation
frameworks.one_line: Strategy linking proximal-state associations to support local wayfinding.

frameworks.name: vector-based navigation
frameworks.one_line: Strategy using directions between distal landmarks to guide travel.

frameworks.name: place/grid cell representation
frameworks.one_line: Mammalian navigational coding compared to learned network units.

core_question: What strategies and representations support goal-directed human navigation in wholly novel environments, and do deep meta-learning agents converge on the same solutions?
key_assumptions: Novel-environment wayfinding requires combining landmark memory with on-the-fly transition information.
Optimal shortest-path policies provide a normative reference for human strategy use.
Artificial agents meta-trained with RL can be compared to humans at strategy and representation levels.
manipulations: Wholly novel (unfamiliar) environments instead of a single well-learned one.
Position along the journey (different stereotyped points within a trial).
Comparison between human participants and meta-RL deep networks on matched tasks.
key_findings: Humans adaptively mix transition-based and vector-based strategies at stereotyped points on a journey.
Meta-RL agents trained for shortest paths show near-identical adaptive strategy mixing.
Network units specialized for vector- vs transition-based strategies emerged during training.
These units' response patterns and representational geometries resemble mammalian navigational systems.
interpretations: Optimal novel-environment navigation relies on an adaptive mix of transition- and vector-based strategies.
Distinct representational modes in the brain support these complementary navigational strategies.
